serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw pork leg steak serving is approximately 150g, representing one steak portion suitable for a single adult meal before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Pork leg steak, raw (F06090026); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, leg (ham), whole, separable lean only, raw (FDC ID 168349 / NDB 10152)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre), sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F06090026 (confidence 0.95). Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g) and trans_fat_g are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168349 (Pork, fresh, leg (ham), whole, separable lean only, raw), an approved secondary source, as HPB does not publish these sub-fractions. Total fat from USDA (~2.17g) is consistent with HPB-reported 2.4g within rounding tolerance. Both HPB and USDA are approved sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pork leg steak, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g carbohydrate per 100g confirmed by HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F06090026). GI measurement requires a fermentable carbohydrate substrate; pure protein and fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response and are excluded from GI classification per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Glycemic load per serving is therefore 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (49mg/100g) and high potassium (380mg/100g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Pork leg steak, raw is suitable for individuals with hypertension when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades; its naturally low sodium content (49mg/100g) and high potassium (380mg/100g) support blood pressure management in line with DASH diet recommendations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; high lean protein (23.3g/100g) with very low fat (2.4g/100g) does not raise postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork leg steak, raw is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains no carbohydrate or sugar and will not elevate blood glucose; its high lean protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance, and it should be grilled or baked without sugary marinades to preserve its low-glycaemic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (2.4g/100g) and low saturated fat (~0.83g/100g) with moderate cholesterol (48mg/100g); lean pork leg is among the lower-fat red meat options
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork leg steak, raw is a lean red meat option with low total fat and low saturated fat, making it acceptable in moderate quantities for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, provided it is prepared by grilling or baking without added saturated fats and consumed as part of a diet rich in vegetables, whole grains, and omega-3 sources.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production, potentially exacerbating joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit pork leg steak to 1–2 servings per week per EULAR dietary guidance, pair it with anti-inflammatory foods such as fatty fish, leafy greens, or turmeric, and prefer grilling or baking to avoid additional pro-inflammatory fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork leg steak, raw, is an uncooked cut of meat taken from the hind leg portion of the pig, typically sold bone-in with lean muscle meat and a small amount of internal and external fat attached. It displays a pale pink colour with a firm, dense texture characteristic of leg muscles that bear significant load during the animal's life. As a raw product, it requires cooking before consumption and is suitable for grilling, baking, pan-frying, or braising. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, very low-fat food providing 23.3g of protein and only 2.4g of fat per 100g, with zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it one of the leaner cuts of pork available. Its energy density is low at 116 kcal per 100g. The sodium content is naturally low at 49mg/100g, and potassium is notably high at 380mg/100g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is moderate at 48mg/100g. Clinically, this cut is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (no glycaemic impact), hypertension (DASH-aligned Na:K ratio), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat). Those with rheumatoid arthritis should moderate red meat intake due to arachidonic acid content. In culinary contexts, pork leg steak is widely used in Western cooking traditions and is also common across Southeast Asian cuisines when marinated and grilled. It is classified as a raw, minimally processed meat product with no allergens and is gluten-free and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F06090026, confidence 0.95 in source record), which is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. Fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced and adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168349 (Pork, fresh, leg (ham), whole, separable lean only, raw), an approved secondary source. All core macros are consistent between sources within rounding tolerance. Record is reviewed and canonical per input provenance.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for raw pork leg steak — this is naturally zero as pork muscle meat contains no digestible carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle product containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168349 for lean pork leg). Saturated_fat_g (0.83g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.07g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.27g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168349 as HPB does not publish fatty acid sub-fractions; total fat cross-checks to approximately 2.17g which is consistent with HPB-reported 2.4g within rounding tolerance. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond those reported by HPB is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this specific entry (F06090026); USDA FoodData Central values for pork leg exist but were not incorporated to avoid source mixing for micronutrients not confirmed in the primary HPB record. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); GI measurement is not meaningful for pure protein and fat foods per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Halal status is set to false as pork is not halal.
